title: Moment of inertia of rigid bodies

concepts.title: Moment of Inertia of a continuous body
concepts.conceptBody:
Moment of Inertia of Continuous Body 

For calculating moment of inertia of a continuous body, we first divide the body into suitably chosen infinitesimal elements. The first choice depends on the symmetry of body. Consider an element of the body at distance $r$ from the axis of rotation. The moment of inertia of this element about the axis we define as $\left( \text{dm} \right){{\text{r}}^{2}}$ and the discrete sum over particles becomes integral over the body :

$\text{I}=\left( \text{dm} \right){{\text{r}}^{2}}$

concepts.title: Moment of Inertia of a uniform thin rod
concepts.conceptBody:
Moment of Inertia of a thin rod

(A) For a uniform thin Rod : 

If we have to find moment of inertia about an axis $\text{AB}$ inclined at an angle $\theta $ to the rod.

Derivation 

Take an element $\text{dx}$ at a distance

Then, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ dm}=\lambda \text{dx},\text{r}=\left( \text{xsin}\theta \right)$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & I=\int ~dI=\int ~\left( dm \right){{r}^{2}} \\ {} & I=\int ~dI=\mathop{\int }_{-L/2}^{L/2}\lambda dx\left( {{x}^{2}}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta \right) \\ {} & I=\left( \frac{M}{L} \right)\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta \left| \frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3} \right|_{-L/2}^{L/2}=\left( \frac{M{{L}^{2}}}{12}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta \right) \\ \end{array}$

About an axis inclined at an angle $\theta $ and passing through its end.

(B) MOI of a non-uniform rod with linear density:

$\lambda=\lambda_0\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{L}}\right)$

$I=\int d I=\int_{-\frac{L}{2}}^{\frac{L}{2}} \lambda_0\left(1+\frac{x}{L}\right) x^2 \sin ^2 \theta d x$

$=\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{L}} \sin ^2 \theta\right)\left[\frac{11 \mathrm{~L}^3}{96}\right]=\frac{11 \mathrm{ML}^2}{96} \sin ^2 \theta$

concepts.title: Moment of Inertia of rectangular lamina
concepts.conceptBody:
Moment of Inertia of rectangular lamina

(A) About an axis parallel to any side and passing through centre. 

(About X-axis)

$\mathrm{dm}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{lb}}\right)(\mathrm{ldy}), \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{y}$

$\therefore \quad \int \mathrm{dI}=\int \mathrm{dm} \mathrm{r}^2$

$\mathrm{I}=\int_{-\mathrm{b} / 2}^{\mathrm{b} / 2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{lb}} \mathrm{dy}\right) \mathrm{y}^2=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{b}}\left|\frac{\mathrm{y}^3}{3}\right|_{-\mathrm{b} / 2}^{\mathrm{b} / 2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{Mb}^2}{12}\right)$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{x}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{Mb}^2}{12}\right) \text {, similarly, } \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{y}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{ML}^2}{12}\right)$

$\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{z}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{Mb}^2}{12}+\frac{\mathrm{ML}^2}{12}\right) \text { (perpendicular axis theorem) }$

$I_z=\frac{M}{12}\left(L^2+b^2\right)$

(B) For a square lamina (of side a) 

${{\text{I}}_{\text{z}}}=\frac{\text{M}}{12}\left( {{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{a}}^{2}} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{M}{{\text{a}}^{2}}}{6} \right)$

concepts.title: Moment of Inertia of circular ring
concepts.conceptBody:
Moment of Inertia of Circular Ring 

(i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}I=\text{dm}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

Consider an element of mass $\text{dm}$ at a distance $\text{R}$ from the centre.

$\therefore \quad I=R^2 \int d m$

$\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{MR}^2$

(ii) MOI of the given cut ring :

$\text{I}=\left( \text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}} \right)$

(remains same as that of complete ring)

(iii) MOI about a diametrical axis.

$I_x+I_y=I_z$

$2 \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{z}}$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{z}}}{2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{2}\right)$

(iv) MOI about a tangential axis parallel to diameter

${{\text{I}}_{1}}=\frac{\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}}{2}+\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}=\frac{3}{2}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

(v) MOI about a tangential axis perpendicular to diameter

$\text{I}_{\text{z}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}+\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}=2\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

concepts.title: Moment of Inertia of circular disc
concepts.conceptBody:
Moment of Inertia of Circular Disc

Consider a ring of thickness $dr$ at a distance $r$ from the centre

$\mathrm{dm}=\sigma(2 \pi \mathrm{r}) \mathrm{dr} \quad \text { where } \sigma=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\pi \mathrm{R}^2}$

$\therefore \quad \int \mathrm{dI}=\int \mathrm{dmr} r^2$

$=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\pi \mathrm{R}^2}(2 \pi) \int_0^{\mathrm{R}} \mathrm{r}^3 \mathrm{dr}$

$\mathrm{I}=\frac{2 \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{R}^2} \times \frac{\mathrm{R}^4}{4}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MR}^2$

$\mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MR}^2$

About a diametrical axis

$I_z=I_x+I_y$

$2 \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{z}}$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{MR}^2$

About a tangential axis

(a) Perpendicular to diameter

$\text{I}=\frac{1}{2}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}+\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}=\frac{3}{2}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

(b) Parallel to diameter

$\text{I}=\frac{1}{4}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}+\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}=\frac{5}{4}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

For a segment of disc

$\text{I}=\frac{1}{2}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

concepts.title: Moment of inertia of spherical shell
concepts.conceptBody:
Moment of inertia of Spherical Shell

To calculate moment of inertia, consider a ring of thickness $\text{Rd}\theta $ making an angle $\theta $ with the horizontal axis.

(i) $\text{dm}=\sigma \left( 2\pi \text{ R sin}\theta \right)\text{Rd}\theta $

$I=\int dI=\int _{0}^{\pi }\left( dm \right){{R}^{2}}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta $, where $\sigma $ is the mass density.

$\sigma =\frac{\text{M}}{4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}}$

$I=\frac{M}{4\pi {{R}^{2}}}\left( 2\pi {{R}^{4}} \right)\int _{0}^{\pi }\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{3}}\theta \right)d\theta $

$=\left( \frac{\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}}{2} \right)\times \left( \frac{4}{3} \right)=\frac{2}{3}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

(ii) About a tangential axis :

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{I} & ~={{\text{I}}_{\text{CM}}}+\text{M}{{\text{d}}^{2}} \\ {} & ~=\frac{2}{3}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}+\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

$\text{I}=\frac{5\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}}{3}$

concepts.title: Moment of inertia of hollow and solid cylinder
concepts.conceptBody:
Moment of inertia of hollow and solid cylinder


(A) Hollow cylinder $=\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$ 
(split it into rings) 
(B) Solid cylinder $=\frac{1}{2}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$ 
(split into discs)

concepts.title: Moment of inertia of solid sphere
concepts.conceptBody:
Moment of inertia of Solid Sphere

To calculate, we consider a hollow sphere of thickness dr at a distance $r$ from the centre. $\text{dm}=\rho \left( 4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)\text{dr}$ where $\rho $ is the mass density

$\rho =\frac{M}{\frac{4}{3}\pi {{R}^{3}}}$

Now, $\text{dI}=\int _{0}^{\text{R}}\frac{2}{3}\text{dm}{{\text{r}}^{2}}$

{Please note that here we put $\frac{2}{3}\text{dm}{{\text{r}}^{2}}$ as this is the moment of inertia of hollow sphere}

$\text{I}=\frac{2}{3}\frac{\left( 4\pi \right)}{\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}}}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{R}}{{\text{r}}^{4}}\text{dr}=\frac{2\text{M}}{{{\text{R}}^{3}}}\times \frac{{{\text{R}}^{5}}}{5}=\frac{2}{5}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

concepts.title: Moment of inertia of a cone
concepts.conceptBody:
Moment of inertia of a cone 

(A) Hollow cone 

Consider a ring with height dy at a distance y from $\text{O}$.

Now thickness of ring will be $\text{dysec}\theta $

also, $\sigma =\frac{\text{M}}{\pi \text{Rl}}$

Hence, $\text{dm}=\sigma \left( 2\pi \text{x} \right)\text{dysec}\theta $

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}I=\left( \text{dm} \right){{\text{x}}^{2}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\int _{0}^{\text{H}}\sigma \left( 2\pi \text{x} \right)\text{dysec}\theta {{\text{x}}^{2}}$

$\text{I}=\frac{\text{M}}{\pi \text{Rl}}\left( 2\pi \right)\text{sec}\theta \int _{0}^{\text{H}}{{\text{x}}^{3}}\text{dy}$

Now, we can see from the figure that $\frac{y}{x}=\frac{H}{R}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=\frac{\text{yR}}{\text{H}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\frac{2\text{M}}{\text{Rlcos}\theta }\int _{0}^{\text{H}}{{\text{y}}^{3}}\frac{{{\text{R}}^{3}}}{{{\text{H}}^{3}}}\text{dy}=\frac{1}{2}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\frac{1}{2}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$

(B) Solid cone 

Consider a disc of thickness dy.

Then

$dm=\rho \left( \pi {{x}^{2}} \right)dy$

Where $\rho =\frac{\text{M}}{\frac{1}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}}\text{H}}$

$I=\int _{0}^{H}\frac{1}{2}\rho \pi {{x}^{4}}dy$

$=\frac{\pi \text{H}}{2\left( \frac{\pi }{3}{{\text{R}}^{2}}\text{H} \right)}\times \mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{H}}\frac{{{\text{y}}^{4}}{{\text{R}}^{4}}}{{{\text{H}}^{4}}}\text{dy}$

$\text{I}=\frac{3}{10}\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}$ 
Here also 
$\frac{\text{x}}{\text{y}}=\frac{\text{R}}{\text{H}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=\text{y}\frac{\text{R}}{\text{H}}$

concepts.title: Radius of Gyration
concepts.conceptBody:
Radius of Gyration 

It is the radius of a uniform circular ring, having same mass as that of body, and with the given axis having as its own axis, such that moment of inertia of ring becomes equal to that of the body.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{I} & ~=\text{M}{{\text{K}}^{2}} \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K} & ~=\sqrt{\frac{\text{I}}{\text{M}}} \\ \end{array}$

Example

An equilateral triangle is formed by 3 identical rods each of mass $m$ and length 1. 

(a) Find the radius of gyration, when axis passes through one vertex and parallel to opposite side. 

(b) Passes through the vertex and perpendicular to the plane of triangle. 

Solution : 

  1. Moment of inertia for rods PQ and PR, $\text{I}=\frac{\text{ml}}{3}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}{{60}^{\circ }}$ and for $\text{rodQR}$

$\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{m}(1 \sin 60)^2 \text { \{as } \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{wr}^2 \text { for this rod\} }$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}_{\text {net }}=2\left(\frac{\mathrm{ml}^2}{3} \sin ^2 60\right)+\mathrm{m}\left(1 \sin 60^{\circ}\right)^2$

$=\frac{\mathrm{ml}^2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4} \times 2+\mathrm{ml}^2\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Now, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\left( 3\text{M} \right){{\text{K}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}=1\sqrt{\frac{5}{12}}$

  1. Now I for rod PQ and PR

$\text{I}=\left( \frac{\text{m}{{\text{l}}^{2}}}{3} \right)$

and I for rod QP

$\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{CM}}+\mathrm{Md}^2$

$=\frac{1}{12} \mathrm{ml}^2+\mathrm{M}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{l}\right)^2$

$=\frac{\mathrm{ml}^2}{12}+\frac{3 \mathrm{ml}^2}{4}$

$\mathrm{I}_{\text {net }}=\frac{8 \mathrm{ml}^2+\mathrm{ml}^2+9 \mathrm{ml}^2}{12}=\frac{18 \mathrm{ml}^2}{12}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{ml}^2$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{K}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{I}}{3 \mathrm{M}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
